For @mountainhardwear, performance and style go hand in hand when it comes to climbing 

What once existed primarily on cliff faces and bouldering walls, as a small, tight-knit community, now influences everything from design and fashion to how people think about movement and adventure. 

Spending decades building alongside athletes, the outdoor brand is bringing that classic climbing feel to the contemporary conversation. With its new “Float On” collection, the staple outdoor brand balances everyday style with technical performance, designing pieces that are made for life on and off the wall. 

Tested through repetition, failure, and success, Mountain Hardwear’s latest line is built to carry you from the base to your personal summit.

Click the link in bio for more.

🎤: @easternspy 
🎥: @y.lanyi + @dillfiles 
Special thanks to: @metrorock_bushwick

posts.caption:
OpenAI may be building the future, but its merch is stuck in 2022.⁠
⁠
The latest Supply Co. drop pairs cursive slogans, logo socks, and a tired Aimé Leon Dore-inspired aesthetic with a campaign that looks suspiciously AI-generated. It’s selling out—but popularity has never been a prerequisite for good taste.

Sliced bread. The wheel. Reese's Peanut Butter Cups. @chanelofficial Pre-Fall/Winter 2026 carpenter jeans. Some inventions simply can't be improved upon, and Matthieu Blazy's clever twist on a workwear classic might just belong in that company. At first glance, they're everything you'd expect from a pair of carpenter jeans: relaxed, practical, and pocket-heavy. Look closer, though, and the magic reveals itself. Just off the left back pocket, Chanel's interlocking CC logo doubles as an impossibly elegant hammer loop, a small detail that quietly steals the show.⁠
⁠
That subtle flourish sums up why Blazy's vision for Chanel feels so compelling. Rather than relying on spectacle, he elevates familiar silhouettes through thoughtful craftsmanship. Carpenter jeans have long evolved from job-site essentials into fashion staples, but Chanel's version refines the formula with a detail so deceptively simple it makes even a $2,800 price tag feel part of the fantasy.⁠

posts.alt_text:
Casa Batlló just opened a part of the building the public has never seen before.⁠
⁠
After more than a century as a private family residence, the last original apartment inside Gaudí’s Barcelona masterpiece has been meticulously restored. Hidden floral stuccoes, undulating ceilings, recycled doors, and even a previously unknown handle design have all been uncovered — revealing another layer of Gaudí’s obsessive attention to detail.⁠

NYC: art’s enfant terrible is in town.⁠
⁠
Last night, @olaoluslawn and @saatchiyates opened the doors to ‘Slawn on W18th,’ his largest show to date. Timed with the World Cup finale, the exhibition is anchored by the 15” x 15” artwork featured on Nigeria’s third kit, alongside several new paintings and football-inspired wooden sculptures.⁠
⁠
Other surprises include a massive collaborative flag piece with designer @asspizza, made from the artist’s canvas scraps, a live performance by @thelonious_art, and an exclusive Slawn x @nike shop stocked with accessories and lifestyle apparel from his Team Nigeria collaboration.⁠
⁠
Head to 456 W18th Street in Chelsea to catch the show before it closes on Sunday, July 19.⁠ Read more via the link in bio.⁠
⁠
Credit: Patty Orem/ Hypebae/ Erin Ikeuchi/ Hypeart/ Courtesy of the artist and Saatchi Yates
posts.alt_text:
“Men do think they’re beautiful,” @mickalenethomas tells @hypebeast in her Brooklyn studio. “It’s okay to show your sensitive side. But that’s not always afforded to some Black men within their own communities.”

The Jersey-born, New York-based artist has spent the last two decades rewriting the rules of Black portraiture. Her bold, rhinestone-encrusted multimedia portraits of women in opulent interiors have lifted her to the top of the contemporary art game. And now, her gaze is turning elsewhere.

In comes ‘Beneath the Moon,’ her first exhibition devoted to Black masculinity. Now up at @theshepherddetroit, here, Thomas reworks an editorial commission into her signature monumental, rewiring canonical motifs into new visions of glamor, agency, and possibility. 

For our latest Hypeart Visits, we caught up with the artist to learn more about her muses, the origin of her rhinestones, and what it means to turn softness into strength. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

Photo: Studio portraits by Lila Barth. All images courtesy of the artist and @librarystreetcollective.

posts.caption:
We made a trip to Tuscany to check out the legacy and process behind @miumiu‘s Matelassé bags firsthand. From the archive that houses its history to the workshop floor where a single leather panel becomes a Wander or perhaps an Arcadie, here's everything we saw along the way.
Credit: Miu Miu / Hypebae
On a Fall/Winter 2006 Paris Fashion Week runway, Miu Miu introduced a leather so distinct in texture and attitude that it would go on to define the house's design vocabulary, the Matelassé technique.
Our visit began with a tour of the Prada Group's industrial headquarters designed by architect Guido Canali. Built primarily from steel, the complex holds a near-equal balance of structure and greenery.
The Matelassé origin story came out of an experiment, and once it hit the runway, it took off in a way no one on the design team fully anticipated at the time.
From the main entrance, we moved into the archive; a room where decades of Miu Miu history live side by side.
The archive holds an estimated 22,100 Miu Miu pieces, including 6,500 handbags.
The design team visits the archive daily for reference, and pieces regularly leave the archive for exhibitions, campaigns and events in Milan and beyond.
The Matelassé origin story came out of an experiment, and once it hit the runway, it took off in a way no one on the design team fully anticipated at the time.
The technique begins with a machine program that stitches across the leather panel, and once it's cut away, an elastic lycra layer underneath pulls the surface inward into the raised, textured pattern the process takes its name from.
Every pattern, every panel, every piece of a Matelassé bag is worked out in 2D design programs before it ever becomes a physical object.
Once cut, all the components for a single bag are boxed together and handed off to an assembly operator. A single Wander bag is built from roughly 100 individual pieces.

Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.caption:
Sahher Bambba (@sahherbambba) is exactly where she’s always wanted to be. From landing her breakthrough role as the unapologetic Karishma Talwar in The Ba**ds of Bollywood to navigating the entertainment industry as an outsider, she believes that what’s meant for her will always find its way. There’s an undeniable energy about her, and we got a glimpse of it during our latest cover shoot.
For our latest Youthquake cover, amidst LSA's official food month, Sahher joined us for a midnight adventure at a sparkly convenience store, where she was quite literally like a kid in a candy store. One moment, she’s climbing into a shopping cart; the next, she’s turning up the heat in the frozen section. Her infectious joy is impossible to miss.
Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.
Editor: Analita Seth (@analitaseth)
Shoot produced by Six Ways to Sunday (@sixwaystosundayofficial_)
Photographer: Sahil Khan (@sssahilkhann) (@sixwaystosundayofficial_)

Produced by: Chirrag Guha (@guha.archie)

Makeup: Pooja Fernandes (@makeupwali)

From landing her breakthrough role as the unapologetic Karishma Talwar in The Ba**ds of Bollywood to navigating the entertainment industry as an outsider, she believes that what’s meant for her will always find its way. There’s an undeniable energy about her, and we got a glimpse of it during our latest cover shoot.

For our latest Youthquake cover, amidst LSA's official food month, Sahher joined us for a midnight adventure at a sparkly convenience store, where she was quite literally like a kid in a candy store. One moment, she’s climbing into a shopping cart; the next, she’s turning up the heat in the frozen section. Her infectious joy is impossible to miss.

Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.

@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.

Who would have thought a Christopher Nolan film would help usher in this summer's latest beauty trend?

Between the gleaming skin, halo braids, and hip-sweeping waves, the press tour for #TheOdyssey seemed to promote Grecian beauty with each stop it took. And the best part about these looks? They’re actually attainable. At the link in bio, InStyle's Beauty Director @lauren_valenti gets into how you can channel your inner goddess all summer long.

Un rovescio a una mano, raro nel tennis di oggi, e un gioco elegante e variato: @Lore_Musetti, 24 anni, ha aperto il 2026 conquistando il quinto posto del ranking ATP, prima che un infortunio alla gamba sinistra lo costringesse a rinunciare al Roland Garros. Global ambassador di #BottegaVeneta, il tennista si racconta a @LOfficielItalia, ritratto dagli scatti di @RaffGrosso e con lo styling di @Fernando.Echeverria_, vestendo un total look della collezione #Fall2026, firmato dalla direttrice creativa @Louise_Trotter_. Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!
The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.
From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.
Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”
Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

posts.caption:
La nuova collaborazione tra @Timberland e @Jacquemus interpreta in chiave giocosa una silhouette intramontabile: la Authentic Boat Shoe si veste di Lagoon Blue, una tonalità audace ispirata all’Art Déco e agli anni ‘80 Memphis che caratterizza la collezione PE 2027 “Le Bonheur” di Jacquemus. Realizzata in pelle pieno fiore Premium, foderata e dotata della suola caratteristica del marchio, la boat shoe rivisita il modello tradizionale con logo dell’albero azzurro e lacci abbinati per una finitura monocromatica essenziale, mentre le puntine metalliche personalizzate richiamano i dettagli distintivi di #Jacquemus. Un’omaggio a un’estate ideale, dove l’artigianalità incontra il design contemporaneo.
Photo: Jacquemus
#Collaborazioni #SummerStyle #LOfficiel #Timberland

posts.caption:
Nel ritrarre #ZoeKravitz nel 2025, su commissione di Anthony Vaccarello per @YSL, #FrancescoClemente ha fatto quello che fa da sempre: dipingere dal vivo, senza mai passare attraverso un’immagine mediata. «C’è il momento dell’incontro, occhi negli occhi, che riesce a cogliere aspetti dell’interiorità, sempre in una dimensione intermedia, che non svela mai», racconta Francesca Pietropaolo, curatrice con Robert Storr di “Francesco Clemente. In Between”, fino al 6 settembre 2026 alla @TriennaleMilano: la prima mostra italiana dell’artista napoletano dopo diciassette anni. Dagli autoritratti agli acquarelli di “Self-Portrait in a Better World”, fino al nomadismo indiano e alla New York di Warhol e Basquiat, Clemente costruisce un sincretismo di misticismo e colore. «Non so costruire, so solo partecipare e lasciare che l’immagine si componga da sola», spiega a @Fabia.DiDrusco sulle pagine de @LOfficielItalia, Luglio/Agosto 2026.
1. “Portrait of Zoë Kravitz, Saint Laurent Summer ‘25, commissionato da Anthony Vaccarello, © Francesco Clemente, foto di Argenis Apolinario, Courtesy of Saint Laurent.
2. @lofficielhommes, n.32, 2013.
4. “Birthday Self Portrait, 2001, © Francesco Clemente, foto di Argenis Apolinario (Courtesy dell’artista e Vito Schnabel Gallery)
5. “Winter Flowers in Spring II”, 2025, Milk paint and ink on canvas115 1/2 x 193 inches (293.4 x 490.2 cm) overall©Francesco Clemente; Photo by Argenis Apolinario (Courtesy dell’artista e Vito Schnabel Gallery)
7. “Porta Coeli”, 1983 © Francesco Clemente (Courtesy dell’artista)
8. Particolare da “Alba”, 2024 © Francesco Clemente, photo di Argenis Apolinario (Courtesy dell’artista)
9, 10. “Francesco Clemente: In Between”, Exhibition view Story of My Country I, 1990 (Foto Delfino Sisto Legnani © Triennale Milano)
#Art

posts.caption:
His work has brought him to countless destinations across the globe, but @TravisScott still has one major stop left on his bucket list. “I hit every continent on the last tour except Antarctica,” he says. “It’s kind of crazy.”
For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.”
Read more about Scott’s adventures at the link in our bio.

posts.alt_text:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!

The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.

From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.

Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”

Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

Global Chief Content Officer @giampietrobaudo

posts.caption:
The 2026 July/August issue is here!
At the center of this year’s Summer Entertainment Issue is @TravisScott, who opens up about working on the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia,’ the travels and experiences fueling his next chapter, and making his acting debut as part of a star-studded ensemble in Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey.’
As much as we love a blockbuster with a stacked A-list cast, championing fresh talent is always top of mind. Inside the issue, we spotlight eight rising stars whose work this summer demands your attention, from ‘Outer Banks’ star @MadisonBaileyBabe to Avantika of ‘Not Suitable for Work’ to @sashacalle of ‘Sugar.’
It’s also a blockbuster time for literary escapes, as the rise of #BookTok turns reading into an unexpected status symbol that’s shaping summer travel. Looking ahead to the Fall collections, we explore the age-old question of whether sex still sells, given the state of undress on the runway, red carpet, and social feeds in 2026. Stay tuned for the issue hitting newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
On the 29th anniversary of Gianni Versace’s passing, we’re revisiting eight design signatures that made him unmistakably him — the eight commandments of @versace, if you will.
At a time when many of his peers favored austere minimalism, #GianniVersace stayed true to himself. His flashy signature looks set him apart from anyone else in the fashion world: bondage-inspired gowns, vibrant prints, and metallic miniskirts. Ever the innovator, when a technique or fabric he wanted didn’t exist, he simply invented it.
From the Medusa logo to the famed ‘90s bondage collection, Gianni’s mark on the house lives on through public memory and the cyclical nature of fashion trends.
Read more on his design signatures at the link in our bio.
Photos Getty Images
#versace #gianniversacearchive #versacearchives

posts.caption:
⁠A new era for @Marni begins with a love letter to its own archive.⁠
⁠
@meryllrogge.personal has officially taken the helm as creative director — and her Fall/Winter 2026 debut at Milan Fashion Week makes her mission clear: not to reproduce Marni's past, but to revive its spirit. The Belgian-born designer, an Antwerp Royal Academy alum and seven-year veteran of the Marc Jacobs studio, spent months in the house's archives before landing on silhouettes defined by natural shoulders and softly dropped waistlines — a nod to the beloved Marni of the late '90s and early 2000s. "I've been a devotee of Marni since my teens," says Rogge. "It is the very landscape of my fashion identity."⁠
⁠
But it was a move to Milan, not the archives, that truly shaped her debut. "I wasn't interested in reproducing Marni's past. I wanted to revive its spirit, its emotional core," she explains. "In Belgium and Northern Europe, people are more relaxed. In Milan, people truly make an effort and dress extremely well every day." Read more from Rogge on her journey, her runway debut, and what's next for Marni — link in bio.⁠
⁠
Writer Simone Vertua⁠
⁠
Photos courtesy of Maciej Kucia and Marni⁠
⁠
#Marni #MeryllRogge

posts.caption:
Why is hypersexual fashion dominating runways, red carpets, and pop culture? Welcome to fashion’s biggest contradiction.
Naked dressing—skimpy, sheer, strategically undone—has quietly gone from scandal to formula. From Gigi Hadid’s sheer @miumiu at the Met to #Demna’s diamond-thonged @Gucci debut, this season’s biggest looks share one thread: skin as statement, not shock value.
Fashion historian @valerie_steele_fashion points to a decade-long shift, noting that hypersexual fashion “has become more and more normalized again in the last maybe 10 years” — with major designers leading the charge. The proof is everywhere: @matthieu_blazy’s subversive see-through mesh @chanelofficial suit, and the bodycon, day-after dishevelment, and diamond thongs that reigned at Gucci’s Fall/Winter 2026 debut.
But here’s the twist — as the red carpet bares more, culture is doing the opposite. Call it a sex recession, or perhaps a shift toward conservatism—but the runway has always existed to reflect or subvert what’s going on off the runway. Read more at the link in our bio.
Writer @jessicakroy
Photos courtesy of Getty Images and HBO

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup Final has officially become the most expensive sporting event ever held in the United States.
With Argentina set to face Spain, ticket prices have reached unprecedented levels, driven by overwhelming global demand, limited availability, and the historic significance of the tournament’s first-ever 48-team edition. Premium hospitality packages have climbed into the millions, while even standard resale tickets are commanding record-breaking prices.
More than just a football match, the 2026 final has become one of the world’s most exclusive live events, where sport, luxury, and global demand have combined to rewrite the record books.
#Luxurious #fifaworldcup2026

posts.caption:
For those who believe luxury should have no limits, this may be the ultimate way to experience the FIFA World Cup Final.
The $1 million package includes a stay in a spectacular private penthouse, helicopter transfers, VIP match access, private butler service, luxury transportation, exclusive dining experiences, and a fully personalized itinerary designed around football’s biggest night. Every detail is curated to deliver an unforgettable World Cup experience with the highest level of comfort, privacy, and exclusivity.
As luxury hospitality continues to evolve, experiences like this prove that for the world’s wealthiest travelers, the journey can be just as extraordinary as the final itself.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Dubai Princess Sheikha Mahra bint Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum recently visited Jacob & Co. for an exclusive high jewelry experience, exploring some of the Maison’s most extraordinary creations.
During her visit, Her Highness personally tried on a selection of Jacob & Co.‘s high jewelry masterpieces, each showcasing exceptional gemstones, intricate craftsmanship, and the bold artistry that has become synonymous with the House. The private presentation highlighted the brand’s commitment to creating one-of-a-kind pieces for the world’s most discerning collectors.
The visit reflects the enduring relationship between royalty and haute joaillerie, where timeless elegance, rarity, and exceptional craftsmanship continue to define the highest expression of luxury.
@_xtianna_ @jacobandco @jacobarabo

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.
For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”
Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.

“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.

“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.

Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

#ManifestCover

Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.

For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”

Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.

Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Starry Union At Gaurav Gupta’s (@gauravguptaofficial) Show Last Night
The couturier unveiled his latest oeuvre with a star-studded fashion show in Mumbai last night. His collection celebrated the idea of union. Drawing inspiration from the eternal relationship between the sun and the moon, it explored the dialogue between lunar and solar energies through bridal couture.
It was a visual treat with stunning, lunar-inspired silhouettes in hues of midnight blue, silver and crystal that met solar expressions rendered in rich reds, molten golds and deep copper tones.
#Manifest #GauravGupta #AnanyaPandey

posts.caption:
For Siddharth Bavishi (@sid_bavishi), weddings transcend mere décor or lavish parties. To him, they are personal milestones. His core philosophy? Guests may love the design, but what stays with them is how a celebration truly made them feel. After all, and we agree, weddings are ultimately about human emotions.
“I’ve always loved the art of bringing people together.” It’s a simple statement, but one that perfectly captures his journey from organising events to building a luxury wedding planning company, The Wedding Trunk (@weddingtrunk).
From expanding the company across India and the UAE to embracing technology, sustainability and conscious celebrations, Siddharth also shares what it really takes to build a lasting career in the business of weddings—and why the glamour seen on social media is only a fraction of the story.
Read the full interview via the link in our bio 🔗
By Srishti Mukherjee (@sri.sshhhhh)
-————
Manifest Approved: An interview with the people shaping the wedding industry, from designers to planners and cultural tastemakers. Each story explores the business, craft and evolving expectations behind modern weddings.
[Luxury Weddings, Wedding Planner, Luxury Weddings Planner, Hospitality, Founder Story]

posts.caption:
Sometimes, all you need is a second to fall in love, and a lifetime to stay that way. And if a destination played Cupid here, Sri Lanka—with its sun-soaked shores, pristine beaches and idyllic views of the coast—would be the ultimate matchmaker.
According to WedMeGood’s Destination Wedding Report 2026, Sri Lanka has emerged as one of the fastest-growing overseas wedding spots, owing to several factors—proximity, luxury hospitality and experience-led celebrations.
We take a look at the lure that this island nation has on the wedding scene.
Read the full story via link in bio🔗

posts.caption:
The soundtrack of the Big Fat Indian Wedding is getting a global upgrade.
Luxury Indian weddings are moving beyond Bollywood medleys, with international DJs, Boiler Room-style stages, immersive live performances and festival-inspired entertainment becoming the new gold standard. From Francis Mercier’s (@francismercier) Afro-house set at a Mumbai wedding to global names like Shimza (@shimza.dj), Hugel (@hugelthug) and Kahani (@kahanimusic), couples are taking inspiration from festivals like Coachella (@coachella), Sunburn (@sunburnfestival) and Ibiza (@ibizaibiza) wedding into an experience.
And it’s not just about who’s behind the console anymore. Traditional DJ booths are being replaced by festival-style main stages, immersive lighting, premium sound systems, live drummers, saxophonists and MCs. Every detail—from talent scouting to technical production—is designed to deliver a world-class show.
Because in 2026, the dance floor isn’t just part of the wedding. It’s the main event.
Read more via the link in bio🔗
By Mirza Shaina Beg (@mirzashainaa)
[Techno, Technomusic, Dance, Rave]

posts.caption:
No maternity clothes? No problem. Gone (mostly) are the days of hiding a baby bump under boxy maternity wear. Instead, expectant mothers are going the Rihanna route: reveal, style and exalt a new, if temporary, silhouette.
There was once a time when showing a pregnant belly was considered scandalous. Coming of age with more body-positive messaging, younger generations have a different attitude toward the pregnant body.
Seeking continuity in their style as their bodies change, many pregnant women are making do with whatever they already have in their closets, or are pulling from their partner's wardrobe. And for those who are fashion-minded, figuring out how to put outfits together out of non-maternity wear — and deciding how to frame, or flaunt, a bump — can be a fun exercise. Swipe through to see how pregnant women are styling their baby bumps, and tap the link in our bio to see more photos. Photos by @dollyfaibyshev
posts.alt_text:
Hats with words stitched across the crown in an inverted arch are ubiquitous in a certain branch of men's fashion. You can buy hats with “Nashville” or “Drunk” or “EBITDA”  or even "Jesus" written upside down.

The capsized cap has particular purchase among pro athletes and those who track them. Steph Curry has worn a cap with “Bay Area” inverted. The golfer Jose Luis Ballester wore an upside down “Sun Devils” hat as he shot his way through the Masters last year. The Cowboys quarterback Dak Prescott is one of many Texas pros to put on the upside-down Dallas hat.

The hats are "more merch than fashion," our style reporter @jacobwgallagher writes in his newsletter. "Their purpose isn’t to look interesting but to shout an interest," he continues. The concept has spread far and wide, but where did the trend start? Read more at the link in our bio and subscribe to The Fashions newsletter. Photos by Noah Graham/NBAE via Getty Images, USA TODAY Sports via Reuters, Andrew Redington/Getty Images

posts.caption:
When most women think of Chanel ballet flats, they think of them in their prim and proper Rue Cambon element. The classic tan-and-black pair have a reputation as polished and Parisian or manicured and uptown.
But recently, a subset of creative, upwardly mobile women are traipsing around in the flats in flashy colors and funky materials. Often in physical jobs and on set for production or styling, these women wear the shoes with baggy cargos, blooming skirts or low-slung jeans, rather than the tweed skirt suits they may have been previously imagined with.
“They’re like a utility shoe,” Ariella Starkman, a creative strategist and producer in New York, said. “They add a level of polish when I’m doing the dirty work,” she added.
Unlike luxury bags, shoes often do not appreciate in value. But Chanel flats appear to be a different story. Tap the link in our bio to read more about how Chanel flats have become the shoe du jour for fashion girls. Photos by Getty Images

posts.caption:
After a couple years of embracing tiny inseams, micro shorts and hot pants, shorts have grown long again. The trend-cycle pendulum has swung all the way back to an 1800s invention: the culotte.
The culotte is, historically and by definition, a trouser masquerading as a skirt. The style gained mainstream appeal in the late 1960s and throughout the ’70s, finding fans in Diana Ross, the model Twiggy and the fashion designer Marc Bohan, who designed a pair made from fur when he was at Christian Dior.
Recently, retailers and brands have introduced silhouettes they describe as culottes, like the long Bermuda shorts from the Gap; Bottega Veneta’s divided A-line skirts; and a trouser whose hem is slowly inching up the leg, ending mid-calf, like the ones sold by Veronica Beard.
Today’s culottes are appealing across demographics and are helping solve a number of fashion crises. Tap the link in our bio to read the history of the culotte and how the style made its return. Photos by Star Max/GC Images, via Getty Images, @simbarashecha, Dave Benett/Getty Images, Estrop/Getty Images

posts.caption:
A reader wrote to our fashion critic: "I have my mother’s satin wedding gown, worn on Oct. 25, 1941 — just six weeks before Pearl Harbor. It’s magnificent, with long sleeves, ruching, satin buttons and a long skirt. I wore it in 1979 and had it preserved, hopefully for a future generation. That has not come to pass, and I struggle with what to do. I can’t see giving it to Salvation Army. Apparently it cannot be dyed. I’ve talked to a tailor about using the skirt material to make one or two blouses. It feels as if it should go into some kind of costume museum, but it’s not a big name designer. Am I being too sentimental?"
Clothes are powerful wormholes to our memories and emotions, which is why it can be so hard to throw them away. But is there liberation in letting go of the past? At the link in our bio, read @vvfriedman's advice. Photo via Jainel Morris

posts.caption:
The summer edition of haute couture isn’t for the faint of heart. It’s always hot outside, and heading indoors seldom provides much relief. Spare a thought for the models, too: Couture looks are often bigger and more complicated than ready-to-wear clothes (the collections shown in July are for the fall season, so bigger, more complicated and heavier).
But in Dior’s actual runway show, the collection felt anything but stifling. There was a similar sensibility in the Schiaparelli collection earlier in the day, but something about the air flow at the Musée Rodin put the lightness front and center. Chanel seemed equally concerned with comfort, with several looks featuring light, sheer tops. At Jean Paul Gaultier, some of the biggest couture-type creations ever sent down a runway were augmented with bare shoulders and bare backs.
See more Paris Couture Week looks from the runway and onlookers at the link in our bio. Visuals by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
At first glance, Look 38 from #PierpaoloPiccioli’s #Balenciaga couture debut may look simple, but it’s an incredible feat of tailoring, textile engineering, and couture pattern cutting.
Instead of separate layers like inner corset, a structural foundation and lining, the engineering is absorbed into one layer of the dress itself. Multiple layers of silk gazar are stacked, topstitched, and shaped to the exact measurements of the wearer, making a gown that is sculptural yet remarkably lightweight.
Rather than relying on rigid corsetry to sculpt the body, the silhouette is created entirely through meticulous tailoring and an intricate internal structure, the type of innovation one expects from modern-day #HauteCouture.
Shoutout to the Balenciaga atelier who worked on this look @mathieugoosse @e.quentin @rvsimond
posts.alt_text:
The #MiddleEast has produced some of the world’s greatest designers, but why do so many come from #Lebanon? Sat at the crossroads of Silk Road trade routes linking Asia, Europe, and Africa, Lebanon developed a tradition of textiles and embroidery, with skills passed down through generations.

Couture is a huge market In the Middle East due to the social culture of weddings and events. While Haute Couture is an institutional system in Paris,  the concept has existed for centuries across India, Pakistan, the Middle East, and China. In the West, couture works as a prestige marketing tool, elsewhere this level of craftsmanship has always been part of how people dress.

Unlike heritage brands which became corporatised and part of conglomerates, Lebanon’s couture boom produced independent designers like #ZuhairMurad, #ElieSaab, and #GeorgesHobeika, who still lead their own houses. Their success built on craftsmanship, personal reputation, and loyal private clients.

Couture is a huge market In the Middle East due to the social culture of weddings and events. While Haute Couture is an institutional system in Paris,  the concept has existed for centuries across India, Pakistan, the Middle East, and China. In the West, couture works as a prestige marketing tool, elsewhere this level of craftsmanship has always been part of how people dress.
Unlike heritage brands which became corporatised and part of conglomerates, Lebanon’s couture boom produced independent designers like #ZuhairMurad, #ElieSaab, and #GeorgesHobeika, who still lead their own houses. Their success built on craftsmanship, personal reputation, and loyal private clients.
posts.alt_text:
At first glance, Look 38 from #PierpaoloPiccioli’s #Balenciaga couture debut may look simple, but it’s an incredible feat of tailoring, textile engineering, and couture pattern cutting.

Instead of separate layers like inner corset, a structural foundation and lining, the engineering is absorbed into one layer of the dress itself. Multiple layers of silk gazar are stacked, topstitched, and shaped to the exact measurements of the wearer, making a gown that is sculptural yet remarkably lightweight.

Rather than relying on rigid corsetry to sculpt the body, the silhouette is created entirely through meticulous tailoring and an intricate internal structure, the type of innovation one expects from modern-day #HauteCouture.

Shoutout to the Balenciaga atelier who worked on this look @mathieugoosse @e.quentin @rvsimond

posts.caption:
#VictoriaBeckham truly is the defining #WAG. She created such a distinct aesthetic that it is still referenced today. Her husband may have been captain but she famously never wore an England #football shirt. Instead opting for slogan tank tops paired with tiny shorts or low-rise jeans, oversized sunglasses, Louboutins and a Birkin.
She knew exactly who she was and rather than dressing like a typical football fan, she dressed like Victoria Beckham: international pop star and an emerging fashion figure. 20 years later and while her style is much more refined, we will never forget the OG wag.

posts.caption:
#OliviaDean’s stylist truly loves her!! and when it came to dressing her biggest tour yet, @simonebeyene worked with some of the best designers and ateliers to reinterpret their collections into custom looks, capturing Olivia’s stage presence, movement, and personal style.
Some of Simone’s favourite looks include the custom Métiers d’Art 2026 #Chanel skirt (slide 5) and the Spring 2026 tasselled #Alaïa dress (slide 2) a fitting farewell to #PieterMulier’s tenure at the house.
The tour saw beautiful couture gowns take centre stage while preserving the warmth and intimacy that have become central to @oliviadeano’s artistic identity. Simone proves that thoughtful styling can be just as powerful a storytelling tool as the music itself.
Images by @lolamansell

posts.caption:
Inside the former chapel of a stately Parisian home lies #RogerVivier’s extraordinary archive. The climate-controlled “laboratory” houses more than 50 years of work by one of the world’s most influential shoe designers.
@rogervivier transformed footwear, pioneering the stiletto heel and the curved “Choc” heel. His work for  #ChristianDior gave shoes the same level of artistry and craftsmanship as Haute Couture. Decades later, his influence continues to shape contemporary fashion, with echoes of his designs still visible at houses including #Dior, #Prada, and #SaintLaurent

posts.caption:
For decades, #Chanel couture shows have ended with a bridal look, but this time #MatthieuBlazy reimagined the traditional narrative as a story of independence. Matthieu was inspired by a book of fairy tales and #GabrielleChanel’s own story, from hardship to success. A wedding dress wasn’t Chanel’s fairytale because she never got married, so instead he closed with the “revenge dress.”
A tribute to Chanel’s life, which was defined by ambition, freedom, and creativity rather than marriage, while famously evoking the spirit of #PrincessDiana, a symbol of confidence and reclaiming one’s own narrative.
